title: The Banditï¿½s Death
content:
To Sir Walter Scott...


O GREAT AND GALLANT SCOTT,
TRUE GENTLEMAN, HEART, BLOOD AND BONE,
I WOULD IT HAD BEEN MY LOT
TO HAVE SEEN THEE, AND HEARD THEE, AND KNOWN.

Sir, do you see this dagger? nay, why do you start aside?
I was not going to stab you, thoï¿½ I am the Banditï¿½s bride.

You have set a price on his head: I may claim it without a lie.
What have I here in the cloth? I will show it you by-and-by.

Sir, I was once a wife. I had one brief summer of bliss.
But the Bandit had wooï¿½d me in vain, and he stabbï¿½d my Piero with this.

And he draggï¿½d me up there to his cave in the mountain, and there one day
He had left his dagger behind him. I found it. I hid it away.

For he reekï¿½d with the blood of Piero; his kisses were red with his crime,
And I cried to the Saints to avenge me. They heard, they bided their time.

In a while I bore him a son, and he loved to dandle the child,
And that was a link between us; but Iï¿½to be reconciled?ï¿½

No, by the Mother of God, thoï¿½ I think I hated him less,
Andï¿½well, if I sinnï¿½d last night, I will find the Priest and confess.

Listen! we three were alone in the dell at the close of the clay.
I was lilting a song to the babe, and it laughï¿½d like a dawn in May.

Then on a sudden we saw your soldiers crossing the ridge,
And he caught my little one from me: we dipt down under the bridge

By the great dead pineï¿½you know itï¿½and heard as we crouchï¿½d below,
The clatter of arms, and voices, and men passing to and fro.

Black was the night when we crept awayï¿½not a star in the skyï¿½
Hushï¿½d as the heart of the grave, till the little one utterï¿½d a cry.

I whisperï¿½d ï¿½give it to me,ï¿½ but he would not answer meï¿½then
He gript it so hard by the throat that the boy never cried again.

We returnï¿½d to his caveï¿½the link was brokenï¿½he sobbï¿½d and he wept,
And cursed himself; then he yawnï¿½d, for the wretch could sleep, and he slept

Ay, till dawn stole into the cave, and a ray red as blood
Glanced on the strangled faceï¿½I could make Sleep Death, if I wouldï¿½

Glared on at the murderï¿½d son, and the murderous father at rest, . . . 
I drove the blade that had slain my husband thrice throï¿½ his breast.

He was loved at least by his dog: it was chainï¿½d, but its horrible yell
ï¿½She has killï¿½d him, has killï¿½d him, has killï¿½d himï¿½ rang out all down throï¿½ the dell,

Till I felt I could end myself too with the daggerï¿½so deafenï¿½d and dazedï¿½
Take it, and save me from it ! I fled. I was all but crazed

With the grief that gnawï¿½d at my heart, and the weight that draggï¿½d at my hand;
But thanks to the Blessed Saints that I came on none of his band;

And the band will be scatterï¿½d now their gallant captain is dead,
For I with this dagger of hisï¿½do you doubt me? Here is his head !
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
